title: How Should Investors Respond To Recent Market Volatility?

Taking a look at futures this morning.
As you can see right now, the Dow is
lower by 49. S&P is up by four and
change. NASDAQ up 25. Tesla shares are
sliding this morning. They missed
earnings estimates. The revenue hit a
record 28 billion dollars after two
straight quarters of decline. Right now,
the stock is down more than 3% in the
pre-market. And there's more earnings
coming out today. We've got Blackstone
uh coming out, American Airlines, Intel,
and Ford. Let's bring in 42 macro
founder and CEO Darius Dale. Darius, uh,
good morning to you. What do you make of
the numbers so far and we did see that
earnings miss for Tesla?
>> Yeah, that was great. I mean, not not
Thank you for having me this morning,
Cheryl. The earnings for Tesla wasn't
great. That's the idiosyncratic risk
factor. From a systematic standpoint, we
are having a fantastic earning season.
In fact, the best we've seen since the
second quarter of 2021. We've got 73% of
S&P 500 companies beating sales and
sales growth accelerating. And we also
have uh 84 85% of companies beating on
earnings which is uh again the best
since the second quarter of 2021.
>> Mark Tapper, jump in here. What what
have you made of the numbers so far?
>> The numbers have been great. I I think
the thing that stands out to me though
is the fact that small cap earnings
growth which has been I believe negative
five of the last six quarters is
actually positive and I think it's up
like 10% as of the last time I checked
yesterday which is great. And then
Darius, my question for you is whether
or not you think this might be an
opportunity despite small caps lagging
over the course of the past probably
decade or so to possibly begin to add
some exposure to small caps given the
fact that estimates for earnings growth
next quarter for small caps is plus 36%.
The quarter after that is around plus
70%. Is there an opportunity there?
>> Yeah, in our view there is. I mean, we
look out into 2026, you know, we
continue to to pound the table on our
paradigm C theme, which we authored back
in April, which essentially calls for
explosive growth in the economy starting
in 2026 as a function of the fiscal
easing we're likely to see, the
substantial monetary easing we're likely
to see, as well as the substantial
deregulation that we're likely to see.
And as a function of that improved
economic backdrop, we're likely to see
investors rotate out of the quote
unquote safety of the kind of MAG 7 AI
type theme, which will still work in our
opinion in absolute terms, but you're
going to see underperforming hedge fund
managers start to like chase small caps
and other factors in the market to try
to play catch-up after a disappointing
year.
>> I want to pivot over to the energy
markets. Uh right now, we are seeing a
massive jump uh in both Brent and WTI
this morning. Both contracts are up more
than 5% uh as you can see on your screen
there to our viewers. And this is this
move is after the Trump administration
slapped further sanctions on Russia's
two largest crude producers. Now they're
blacklisting Ros and Luke Oil. They're
citing Moscow's lack of commitment to
ending the war in Ukraine. This is a
massive policy reversal. President Trump
canceled a planned meeting with Vladimir
Putin and the Treasury Department is
calling it a push for an immediate
ceasefire, warning that more action
could follow if Russia does not change
their course. And these are threatening
to disrupt these sanctions, Russian oil
flows to India and to China. This is two
of the biggest customers that Russia and
Vladimir Putin have right now.
Obviously, when you see these contracts
go up though, that risks the energy
picture domestically for us.
>> Uh, yes it does. But in in reality, I
mean, there's a continued to be a supply
glut in the global energy market. You
have ships floating, the most amount of
oil just floating around in the in
global oceans right now that we've seen
since uh 2020 when oil prices went
negative. If you want to think about the
inflation picture right now, it's
actually quite tame uh in the context of
the uh the continued degradation we're
seeing in the labor market, which has
become the driving factor for Fed
policy. And so if you think about the
the delay the you guys were talking
about the government shutdown earlier in
terms of Mark said it was about 20 basis
points off GDP every week. Uh if the
longer we go with the shutdown the more
likely it is that we see a pull forward
of monetary easing as it relates to the
Fed for the reaction function uh to the
labor market. And so all this in our
opinion is continues to be incredibly
bullish. This is one of the best
investing environments I've seen in my
career. Uh we've obviously benefited
tremendously from that in terms of our
global investor community.
>> Sure. Sure. Sure. And obviously we'll
see how the energy stocks themselves
fare today with this move in in oil. You
know, we'll be watching, you know, Oxy
and Chevron and and Exxon. Let's take a
look at the tenure. You mentioned the
Fed. I want to get into this with you
because we've been watching obviously
that tenure. We are again today we are
below that 4% level for a second
straight day. The Fed though losing
access to private jobs data from payroll
giant ADP just as the government
shutdown is stalling our economic
reports. So ADP ended its data sharing
shortly after a speech by Fed Governor
Christopher Waller and Fed Chair J. Pal
is urging ADP restore access. He's
warning the Fed now has doesn't does not
have frankly any type of timely read on
the employment picture which really
dictates their decision-m which is next
week. Uh also we are going to get
September CPI. That report's going to
come out tomorrow. Here are the
expectations. monthly rise of 410 of a
percent 3.1% year-over-year Darius is
but we've had several guests this week
already tell us they're a little
concerned about the data and the
validity of the data on the CPI report
and then also you need to respond to
what's going on with the jobs data and
these comments from PAL I mean this
really handicaps them for their meeting
>> yeah in some respects it does I mean
obviously the gold standard uh and it's
increasingly less gold but the gold
standard has been the BLS uh report that
we the jobs report that we get every
month but the reality is there are
plenty of other alternative data sources
including those that come from the Fed.
The New York Fed has several surveys of
the labor market. Uh we also have the
beige book which obviously continues to
trend in a negative direction. And if
you just think about the the the context
of the Fed's reaction function, if we
did not receive any new data that should
change their uh that should not change
their trajectory of of policy. The
reality is this is a labor market as a
because of their uh uh you know
sleuthfooted reaction function. This is
a labor market that is very very weak
from our perspective. If you think about
the percent of jobs that are actually
coming from the private sector in terms
of job growth, it's like in the single
digit teens range. Uh and if you think
about the we actually have negative
payroll growth for the true private
services sector and the true uh private
uh manufacturing sector, things that are
not tethered to the government. So the
reality is this is a labor market that
continues to slow down and the Fed needs
to react to it.
>> All right, Darius Dale. We'll see what
they do. We'll be right back, everybody.
